text.title: Decision Log Reconstruction From Git Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Decision Log Reconstruction From Git application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Every reconstructed decision MUST triangulate at least two of: commit message body, PR description, linked ticket. Single-source decisions are flagged 'unconfirmed' and held out of the main log.
rule.rationale: Single-source reconstruction yields high false-positive rates (a commit may describe a refactor that happens to coincide with the decision); triangulation is the standard archaeology bias control.

rule.statement: The detector MUST emit only decisions that fit the ADR shape: identifiable context + at least one rejected alternative + concrete consequences. Bare 'we did X' commits are skipped.
rule.rationale: Including bare changes pollutes the log with code-change records rather than decision records; the receiver cannot tell signal from churn.

rule.statement: Decisions that recur across >=3 commits (e.g. 'use repository pattern') MUST be collapsed into one ADR with a commits list; per-commit duplicates are rejected.
rule.rationale: Without clustering, the log grows linearly with commit count and becomes unusable; clustering compresses repeat decisions into one entry per topic.

rule.statement: Each reconstructed ADR MUST be signed off by a named original-author or current-maintainer. Un-signed ADRs are emitted with status='proposed' and excluded from the published log.
rule.rationale: LLM reconstruction is plausible but often wrong about intent; signoff is the falsifier.

rule.statement: Every ADR MUST embed the originating commit SHAs and PR numbers. ADRs without back-links are rejected.
rule.rationale: Without back-links the ADR is hearsay; the SHA is the proof the receiver can re-read in 2 years.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
